pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Kep Archipelago Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Conservationists are working with local communities to repair degraded marine habitats in Cambodia. With support from the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, they are restoring seagrass meadows to bring the ocean’s largest vegetarian back to Cambodia’s waters.

pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Kep Archipelago Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Conservationists are working with local communities to repair degraded marine habitats in Cambodia. With support from the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, they are restoring seagrass meadows to bring the ocean’s largest vegetarian back to Cambodia’s waters.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Kep Archipelago Hope Spot

metadescription.value: Conservationists in Cambodia are working to revive the Kep Archipelago's seagrass meadows and, in turn, bring back threatened marine life. More on rolex.org.

text.value: The tireless efforts of marine conservationists restoring fragile seagrass meadows around Cambodia’s Kep Archipelago have paid off, as charismatic marine animals such as dugongs and green turtles return to these idyllic waters.

The dedicated team from Marine Conservation Cambodia (MCC) has applied artificial reefs to restore the ecosystem while deterring harmful fishing practices.

Its success in restoring seagrass meadows and preserving sensitive marine species was recognized by Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative partner Mission Blue, which has added the Kep Archipelago to its network of Hope Spots. They are special places, scientifically identified as critical to the health of the ocean, which are championed by local conservationists who receive communications and scientific support.

Cambodia’s seagrass meadows are among the largest remaining in south-east Asia and provide a vital habitat for many threatened and endangered species. The charming dugong – the ocean’s largest herbivore – relies on the underwater meadows to graze.
After seven years of just letting the ocean breathe a little bit, the ecosystem has started to recover.

“Seagrass is the superhero of the oceans,” says Rachana Thap, Executive Director of MCC and Hope Spot Champion for the Kep Archipelago. These vast underwater grasslands release oxygen and are crucial carbon stores, capable of taking up more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than any forest on land.

Sadly, these delicate habitats have been severely damaged by industrial fishing methods such as bottom trawling, which can wipe out swathes of seagrass in a matter of minutes. Trawlers are “very destructive” to marine life, Thap explains.

Conservationist Paul Ferber founded MCC on Koh Rong Samloem in 2008 with the aim of restoring marine ecosystems, deterring illegal and unregulated fishing, and improving livelihoods for local communities.

MCC started “from the heart and the love of the oceans”, Thap says. She joined the not-for-profit organization as an intern during her undergraduate studies and quickly became an integral part of the team, devising an innovative artificial reef approach as part of her final year project. In 2016, MCC began installing artificial reefs along the seafloor of the Kep Archipelago, a group of a dozen islands southeast of the Cambodian mainland. These structures play a dual function in ecosystem recovery. They provide a surface for creatures such as corals and sponges to colonize and shield the habitat from trawling, allowing time for the marine life to repopulate and grow.

In 2018, the Cambodian government declared the country’s second Marine Fisheries Management Area (MFMA) around the archipelago, banning trawling and other harmful fishing methods in the area.

This MFMA covers more than 11,000 hectares of coral reefs, mangroves and seagrass meadows that provide important feeding grounds and migration routes for many threatened species, including the Irrawaddy dolphin, green and hawksbill turtles, whale sharks, giant clams and seahorses.

Under MCC’s careful watch, Kep’s marine ecosystems have rebounded quickly. The team has seen 1,000 hectares of regrown seagrass meadows within the MFMA. In 2022, it was rewarded with a first glimpse of dugongs – a mother and calf. Since then, dugong sightings have become more regular. Acoustic monitoring in the artificial reefs has also revealed that large pods of dolphins are feeding in the protected area at night. “After seven years of just letting the ocean breathe a little bit, the ecosystem has started to recover,” Thap says.

MCC Island Manager Tanguy Freneat was surprised by the speed of transformation. “I always assumed the environment would take ages to recover from human impact. Seeing it recover with my own eyes gives me a lot of hope for the ocean and the world in general.”
To reduce pressure on this ecosystem, you need to provide alternative livelihoods to the community that relies on it.

Before MCC started working in the Kep Archipelago, many fish populations were nearing collapse. With the protection of the MFMA and the artificial reefs, marine habitats have rebounded, and local communities using sustainable fishing practices have seen their catches increase. “You can see the excitement it brings them – it’s really inspiring,” says Freneat.

By demonstrating the benefits of conservation, Thap has been able to collaborate with coastal communities that rely on the health of these ecosystems for their livelihoods. Her goal is to inspire locals to understand and love their marine environment and want to conserve it, “so they can pass it on to the younger generations”.

Thap has recruited local fishing communities to assist with marine mammal surveys, which have found Irrawaddy dolphins in the waters around the Kep Archipelago. Animals such as dugongs and dolphins are “indicators of how well the ecosystem is recovering”, she comments.

The MCC team is inspiring coastal fishing communities to transition to more sustainable livelihoods, such as oyster farming. It provides bamboo rafts that can be used to grow and harvest shellfish, helping to increase oyster populations that filter pollutants from the water. “To reduce pressure on this ecosystem, you need to provide alternative livelihoods to the community that relies on it,” says Freneat.

Collaborating with local people is key to MCC’s success. Not only does it offer fishing communities an alternative source of income, but they, in turn, contribute unique local knowledge. “Their understanding of the Cambodian ocean makes them the best people to protect it,” Freneat explains.

Seeing the clear efficacy of Thap’s artificial reefs, the Cambodian government has contracted MCC to install 5,000 artificial reefs all along Cambodia’s coastline over the next five years. “It's going to change the whole Cambodian coastal province,” says Thap. “The potential for restoration and returning wildlife is enormous.”

title.value: Watching over the Earth’s blue heart

text.value: Launched in 2009, Mission Blue’s aim is to create a global network of Hope Spots, which are ecologically important areas of the oceans considered vital to the preservation of species, or places where communities rely on a healthy marine environment to survive. So far, Mission Blue has created more than 160 Hope Spots around the world. Key to the programme is the empowerment of local people to make change.

pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - Exmouth Gulf and Ningaloo Coast Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Australia’s Ningaloo Coast teems with marine life, but its survival depends on the biodiverse Exmouth Gulf nearby.

metadescription.value: Hope Spot Champion Ben Fitzpatrick is working to protect Australia's Exmouth Gulf and demonstrate its crucial links to Ningaloo Coast. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Malpelo Island Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Sandra Bessudo has dedicated her life to the wild waters of Malpelo Island. Today, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative and Mission Blue, Bessudo is working tirelessly to help improve and expand the marine protection around Malpelo to preserve its breathtaking biodiversity.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Malpelo Island Hope Spot

metadescription.value: Hope Spot Champion Sandra Bessudo is working to help expand marine protection around Malpelo Island to preserve its biodiversity. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Galápagos Islands Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex is supporting ocean conservation non-profit Mission Blue in its goal to create a global network of Hope Spots, areas that deserve protection as they are home to vital marine ecosystems.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Galápagos Islands Hope Spot

metadescription.value: The waters of the Galápagos Islands, named a Hope Spot by Mission Blue, are a unique living laboratory of diverse endemic species. More on rolex.org.
